item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as it comes from here with no indication it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked it, but mostly because of the test logs. The description was a little boring, but all in all, the article was interesting. The test logs were great except that it some effects didn't seem connected to the object in the picture. Like the apple one and the day of the dead skull interested me because the effects they had were clever, but a few others didn't seem connected and were more like oh shit youre dead. I liked the build up to the epsilon stuff, but I'm not a fan of superpowers. Maybe if there was any sort of downside for the D-class it would have been a more interesting end.
Nevertheless, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Some of the effects are interesting, but it just seems to be a random meme generator. Made me realise how stupid one of my ideas was (A book of memes… I'm fucking serious) >.<

It either needs more fine control (like a switch on the device that allows you to choose the type of hazard) or an easily predictable pattern by which the device amplifies the sensations evoked by particular focal points of the picture.

Not bad, but a little simple at this stage. Down vote sorry >.<

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. Good point, I was going to make it.

2. Possibly meant to say energy density or something similar?

3. I actually really fell for it, what do you do in an emergency? He could've thought it was a terrorist attack :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ehh, I don't really like it. The Granny Smith apple and stop sign were kind of interesting, but then it just turned into "random thing what kills you and sometimes makes you go crazy and eat yourself."

Downvote, sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Materials representing an Epsilon Class cognitohazard must be locked within two steel containers

That's ambiguous. Does it mean: 1) there's two specific steel containers, and only those two specific containers may be used, or 2) there's one steel container inside a second steel container, and the materials must be stored in the inner container?

Research staff have determined that when light enters this device, it subtly alters the wavelength and intensity of individual photons

Individual photons only have a wavelength; intensity is caused by the number of photons.

The buyer surrendered the camera to local law enforcement after taking a photograph that caused him to vomit uncontrollably.

Seems strange to me that he'd turn it over to the police. If he was going to turn it over to anyone, why not some scientists?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Two nitpicks. It's a thing what drives you insane, yet the results are interesting enough for me to let it slide.

Second, really? The D-class got away and remain at large? Bullshit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this SCP as well. I agree with the idea of open test logs, as well as perhaps occasional updates on how the Foundation is doing on locating the two anomalous individuals who are still at large. (I'd also like to learn more about this woman in the cognitohazardous picture, if that's possible.)

Personal tilt aside, it's clear about what the object is and what it's capable of, and far's I'm concerned, it flows well.

+1, and looking forward to future developments!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this, and I'm pretty certain I'll like it a bunch more with a (well-moderated) open testing log. +1 and crossing my fingers for that log.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really thought the concept was cool, yet I can't peg down exactly which part I like best.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i believe Addendum B provides a necessary extension to the otherwise simple concept. Others liked it, so I am hesitant to trim it. I could add a bit though to tie it to the story better, I suppose.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The rest of the article didn't do much for me (felt too "listy", the bit about a cognitohazard causing instacancer, the "throw all the class Ds" style of research), but that ending really tied it together. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am glad that you clarified the testing log so that the progression of objects flows much smoother. As it is, I think it's worth an upvote, but the addendum still rubs me the wrong way, as it doesn't induce a feeling of curiosity and mystery so much as a feeling of cynical "is this actually relevant to the story or is just here for spooky".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry I didn't get to this before ye posted it, but I do like it. Like Anax said, it's giving me an old-school object vibe, focusing on a really original and interesting effect to the fullest degree rather than using it for a story. I would say that it took me a couple reads of the opening paragraph to understand exactly what was going on, but that was more on my side than yours.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The immediate redirection to Addendum B is kinda silly and time-wastey. "Whoops! The information you want isn't in this collapsible, it's in the one immediately below this. Try again!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I definitely want to make the test logs open to new entries, and I'll make an addendum document for that, assuming this succeeds as an article.

I'll edit that test you don't think works. Let me know if there's anything else you see that could be improved.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I sorta like this, even if it is lacking story. It has a good old-school feel, and I almost think the test logs should be made open to new entries.

One test I don't think works is this:

All viewers developed cancerous masses in various parts of their bodies. Additionally, D-2626-19 tackled and held down D-2626-34, who attempted to fight him off as [DATA EXPUNGED] terminated by security personnel.

I don't buy this as a cognitohazardous effect. It's too far beyond the range of something that's affecting the brain.
